steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content: On the left: “an Elvis taxon is not the real thing” is an independent clause. On the right: the blank, followed by “is a misidentified look-alike,” is also an independent clause. The blank must include “however” (a conjunctive adverb) and “it” (the subject of the second clause).

steps.title: Apply the rule for conjunctive adverbs between independent clauses
steps.content:
A conjunctive adverb joining two independent clauses requires either a semicolon before it and comma after (IC; however, IC) or a period to end one sentence and begin another (IC. However, IC). Choice D uses the sentence-boundary pattern: the period after “however” ends the first sentence, and “It” (capitalized) begins the second, properly separating the independent clauses. Choices A, B, and C either redundantly pair “however” with “but,” omit punctuation after the adverb, or use only a comma—each violating the standard punctuation rule (which requires a semicolon or period, not just a comma).

That matches choice D — D is correct.
